ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for baked oysters with truffle togarashi seasoning; closest parent 'oyster food product' used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW (Oysters) used as closest parent; no specific code for baked oysters with truffle togarashi seasoning.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT code 226955003 (Oyster substance) used as closest parent; no specific concept for this preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 12 baked oyster pieces, estimated at approximately 240 g total (approximately 20 g per piece including shell-off oyster flesh and toppings).

nutrient_source.title: Oysters, eastern, wild, cooked, dry heat (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #15169) combined with mayonnaise and truffle oil component estimation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base oyster values derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked eastern oysters (dry heat). Truffle oil, mayonnaise, garlic, and togarashi contributions estimated using USDA FoodData Central component data for each ingredient. Final per-100g values represent a weighted composite of approximately 60% oyster flesh, 20% mayonnaise, 10% truffle oil, 5% garlic, and 5% togarashi spice blend, reflecting a typical baked oyster preparation with these toppings.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — shellfish and protein-dominant foods
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for baked oysters with truffle togarashi. GI estimated at 5 based on analogous high-protein, low-carbohydrate shellfish foods from the University of Sydney GI Database. Oysters and similar shellfish preparations are classified as very low GI due to minimal digestible carbohydrate content. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (5 × 16.3) / 100 ≈ 0.8, where carbohydrate per 240 g serving ≈ 16.3 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from mayonnaise, togarashi seasoning, and natural oyster sodium
hypertension.guidance: Each 240 g serving provides approximately 744 mg sodium, representing about 32% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and avoid additional high-sodium condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and low net carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 5 and a glycemic load of under 1 per serving, baked truffle togarashi oysters have minimal impact on postprandial blood glucose and are generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol content from oysters and mayonnaise, partially offset by beneficial omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Oysters are a source of omega-3 fatty acids and zinc which may support cardiovascular health, but the mayonnaise component adds saturated fat and cholesterol; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation and be mindful of the sodium content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Baked Oysters with Truffle Togarashi (12 Pieces) is a Japanese-influenced seafood dish served at The Oyster Bank restaurant in Singapore. The dish consists of twelve fresh oysters baked in their half-shells and topped with a rich combination of truffle oil, Japanese mayonnaise, togarashi (a Japanese seven-spice blend containing chilli, sesame, orange peel, ginger, and nori), and garlic. The baking process creates a warm, creamy, and slightly spicy topping over the briny, tender oyster flesh. The truffle oil imparts an earthy, luxurious aroma, while the togarashi adds mild heat and complexity. Nutritionally, oysters are one of the richest dietary sources of zinc and vitamin B12, and also provide significant iron, selenium, and omega-3 fatty acids. The mayonnaise and truffle oil contribute to the fat content, making this a moderately energy-dense dish. The glycemic index is very low due to the minimal carbohydrate content, making it suitable for blood glucose management. Sodium is a consideration due to the seasoning components. This dish is relevant for clinical dietary counselling in the context of zinc deficiency, anaemia (iron), and cardiovascular health (omega-3 content versus saturated fat from mayonnaise). It is not suitable for shellfish-allergic individuals or those avoiding eggs.

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry exists in any approved primary source for this specific restaurant preparation. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for cooked oysters, mayonnaise, truffle oil, garlic, and togarashi, weighted by estimated ingredient proportions. GI is analogously estimated from University of Sydney GI Database shellfish data. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved databases and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Nutrient values are composite estimates based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked eastern oysters and individual condiment components (mayonnaise, truffle oil, garlic, togarashi). No direct laboratory analysis or HPB Singapore Foodo entry exists for this specific dish. Serving size of 240 g is estimated at 20 g per piece (shell-off oyster plus toppings) × 12 pieces. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA component data and are not individually confirmed by a single source entry. Halal status is uncertain as oysters are considered halal by most Sunni schools but the restaurant context and mayonnaise sourcing are unconfirmed. Togarashi may contain sesame and soy, making allergen declarations approximate. The cuisine_tags 'Alcoholic Drink' and 'Japanese Rice Bowl' in the input appear to be field survey platform category artefacts and have been disregarded as they do not describe this dish. Price range SGD 35.07–68.66 from input is preserved as contextual metadata only and is not used in nutritional calculations. Multilingual names for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are literal translations of the key components of this fusion dish, as no established local names exist for this specific preparation.
